claim: Women with a higher birth weight are more likely to develop breast cancer later in life.

result.claim: Women with a higher birth weight are more likely to develop breast cancer later in life.
result.reasoning: Multiple systematic reviews and re-analyses of individual participant data consistently indicate a positive association between higher birth weight and an increased risk of breast cancer later in life. Paper 23557241, a systematic review and meta-analysis, found that increased birthweight was associated with a relative risk of 1.15 (95% CI 1.09-1.21) for breast cancer. Paper 16322674, a re-analysis of individual participant data from 32 studies, reported a pooled relative risk of 1.06 per one standard deviation increment in birth weight and a risk of 1.12 for those weighing >= 4.000 kg compared to 3.000-3.499 kg. Paper 27123743, a review of 26 research papers, found a combined relative risk estimate of 1.23 (95% CI 1.13-1.34) for breast cancer when comparing women with high birthweight to those with low birthweight. While paper 37480103 noted an increased risk before adjusting for placental weight, this association was not significant after adjustment, suggesting potential confounding. However, the overwhelming evidence from the other high-quality studies supports the claim.

evidence.text: Higher maternal age was associated with a relative risk of 1.13 (1.02-1.25) for breast cancer.
evidence.source: Intrauterine factors and risk of breast cancer: a systematic review and meta-analysis of current evidence.

evidence.text: Increased birth length was associated with a relative risk of 1.28 (1.11-1.48) for breast cancer.
evidence.source: Intrauterine factors and risk of breast cancer: a systematic review and meta-analysis of current evidence.

evidence.text: Female prenatal experience is associated with the risk of developing breast cancer.
evidence.source: Intrauterine factors and risk of breast cancer: a systematic review and meta-analysis of current evidence.

evidence.text: Compared with women who had placentas weighing less than 500 g in 2 consecutive pregnancies, the risk of breast cancer was increased among women whose placentas weighed between 500 and 699 g in their first pregnancy and at least 700 g in their second pregnancy (or vice versa).
evidence.source: Pregnancy characteristics and maternal risk of breast cancer.

evidence.text: The adjusted hazard ratio for breast cancer risk was 1.10 (95% CI, 0.76-1.59) for those with a high birth weight (> or =4000 g) in 2 successive births.
evidence.source: Pregnancy characteristics and maternal risk of breast cancer.

evidence.text: The corresponding risk of breast cancer was doubled among women whose placentas weighed at least 700 g in both pregnancies.
evidence.source: Pregnancy characteristics and maternal risk of breast cancer.

evidence.text: Birth size might be a correlate of subsequent breast cancer risk.
evidence.source: Birth Size and Breast Cancer Risk: Re-analysis of Individual Participant Data from 32 Studies

evidence.text: Birth weight was positively associated with breast cancer risk in studies based on birth records.
evidence.source: Birth Size and Breast Cancer Risk: Re-analysis of Individual Participant Data from 32 Studies

evidence.text: Breast cancer risk was positively associated with birth length.
evidence.source: Birth Size and Breast Cancer Risk: Re-analysis of Individual Participant Data from 32 Studies

evidence.text: The relative risk estimate for breast cancer comparing women with high birthweight to women with low birthweight combining all studies including both pre- and postmenopausal breast cancer was 1.23 with a 95% confidence interval of 1.13 to 1.34.
evidence.source: Role of birthweight in the etiology of breast cancer.

evidence.text: Epidemiological studies have shown inconsistent findings regarding birth size and breast cancer risk.
evidence.source: Birth Size and Breast Cancer Risk: Re-analysis of Individual Participant Data from 32 Studies

evidence.text: Twenty-six research papers have addressed the association between birthweight and the risk of breast cancer.
evidence.source: Role of birthweight in the etiology of breast cancer.

evidence.text: The cumulative incidence of breast cancer per 100 women by age 80 years was 10.0 in those in the bottom fourth of the birth length distribution.
evidence.source: Birth Size and Breast Cancer Risk: Re-analysis of Individual Participant Data from 32 Studies

evidence.text: A high birth weight (> or =4000 g) in 2 successive births was associated with an increased risk of breast cancer before adjusting for placental weight and other covariates.
evidence.source: Pregnancy characteristics and maternal risk of breast cancer.

evidence.text: Birth length is an independent correlate of breast cancer risk in adulthood.
evidence.source: Birth Size and Breast Cancer Risk: Re-analysis of Individual Participant Data from 32 Studies

evidence.text: Increased birthweight was associated with a relative risk of 1.15 (95% CI 1.09-1.21) for breast cancer.
evidence.source: Intrauterine factors and risk of breast cancer: a systematic review and meta-analysis of current evidence.

evidence.text: The cumulative incidence of breast cancer per 100 women by age 80 years was 10.0 in those in the second fourth of the birth length distribution.
evidence.source: Birth Size and Breast Cancer Risk: Re-analysis of Individual Participant Data from 32 Studies

evidence.text: The cumulative incidence of breast cancer per 100 women by age 80 years was 11.5 in those in the top fourth of the birth length distribution.
evidence.source: Birth Size and Breast Cancer Risk: Re-analysis of Individual Participant Data from 32 Studies

evidence.text: Breast cancer risk was positively associated with head circumference from birth records.
evidence.source: Birth Size and Breast Cancer Risk: Re-analysis of Individual Participant Data from 32 Studies

evidence.text: The cumulative incidence of breast cancer per 100 women by age 80 years was 10.4 in those in the third fourth of the birth length distribution.
evidence.source: Birth Size and Breast Cancer Risk: Re-analysis of Individual Participant Data from 32 Studies
